questions.question: Why cannot a single origin server serve 50M concurrent video viewers directly, even with high bandwidth?
options: A single server's network bandwidth and connection limits are orders of magnitude below what 50M concurrent streams require; CDN edge distribution is the only practical solution.
Copyright law requires video to be distributed from multiple jurisdictions.
Origin servers cannot generate HLS manifests fast enough to serve 50M viewers.
Video encoding requires GPU hardware that is only available at CDN edges.
questions.explanation: 50M viewers × 2 Mbps = 100 Tbps of egress — far beyond any single server's capacity. Only a geographically distributed CDN can serve this.

questions.question: What fundamental difference makes live streaming harder to cache than on-demand video?
options: Live segments do not exist until they are produced, so the CDN cannot pre-warm them; only the most recent N segments are cacheable.
Live video uses a proprietary codec that CDNs do not support.
On-demand video is served from a single region while live video must come from multiple ingest points.
Live streaming clients require WebSocket connections that bypass CDN caching layers.
questions.explanation: On-demand content is fully materialized before first playback; CDN can cache aggressively. Live segments arrive moments before viewers request them, leaving only a small window for cache warming.

questions.question: A streamer's encoder crashes during a popular stream. What should the ingest service and HLS distribution layer do?
options: Hold the last-known manifest segments, wait for reconnect within a timeout, and resume numbering from the next sequence when the streamer reconnects.
Delete the stream record immediately and notify all viewers that the stream has ended.
Replay the last 60 seconds of segments in a loop until the streamer reconnects.
Transcode a black frame video to keep the manifest active during the outage.
questions.explanation: Holding the manifest for a grace period allows normal encoder crashes and network drops to recover transparently. The sequence number continues forward to prevent CDN cache poisoning.

questions.question: Why is GPU-accelerated transcoding necessary for a platform with 500K concurrent live streams?
options: Real-time transcoding of 500K streams into 4 renditions each would require roughly 2M CPU cores at CPU-only throughput, while GPUs cut this by an order of magnitude.
GPU transcoding produces higher quality video than CPU transcoding at the same bitrate.
CDNs only accept video encoded on GPU hardware.
GPU transcoding eliminates the need for a separate transcode worker cluster.
questions.explanation: At 500K streams × 4 renditions, CPU transcoding requires enormous core counts. GPU acceleration makes real-time transcoding economically viable.

questions.question: Chat fan-out for a stream with 1M concurrent viewers at 100 messages per second requires 100M deliveries per second. What architecture pattern handles this?
options: Kafka topic per stream consumed by WebSocket gateway workers that each maintain a shard of viewer connections.
Direct HTTP push from the chat API server to each viewer's connection concurrently.
Polling the chat database from each viewer's client every 500ms.
A relational database trigger that writes one row per viewer per message.
questions.explanation: Kafka-based fan-out decouples message production from delivery and allows WebSocket gateway workers to scale horizontally, each handling a shard of the viewer connection pool.

questions.question: Which metric is the best indicator that live streaming quality is degraded for viewers before they report it?
options: CDN cache hit rate per stream rendition — a drop means viewers are hitting origin directly, which cannot sustain the load.
Total number of active encoders connected to the ingest fleet.
Average segment file size across all active streams.
Number of unique stream keys created in the last 24 hours.
questions.explanation: CDN cache miss rate is the leading indicator of delivery degradation: when it drops, origin load spikes, segment fetch latency increases, and viewers experience rebuffering before they report issues.

questions.question: What is the core trade-off between HLS-based streaming (10-30s latency) and WebRTC-based streaming (sub-1s latency)?
options: HLS is CDN-cacheable and scales to millions of viewers per stream; WebRTC requires peer-to-peer or SFU relay that cannot economically scale past tens of thousands of viewers.
WebRTC produces higher video quality than HLS at the same bitrate.
HLS requires the streamer to have a professional encoder while WebRTC works from a browser.
WebRTC segments are smaller than HLS segments, which reduces CDN storage costs.
questions.explanation: WebRTC's real-time relay model (SFU) is economically impractical for 1M viewer streams. HLS's segment-based pull model is CDN-native and scales to any viewer count.

questions.question: A live stream has 500K concurrent viewers and the CDN edge POPs serving a specific region are overloaded. What is the correct mitigation?
options: The CDN load balancer redirects new viewer connections to alternative edge POPs in the same region, distributing load across the available capacity.
The platform stops accepting new viewers for the stream until the overloaded POPs recover.
The transcode pipeline reduces the number of rendition variants to reduce bandwidth.
Viewers in the overloaded region are automatically switched to lower-bitrate streams at the CDN layer.
questions.explanation: CDN load balancing across POPs is the standard mitigation: redirect overflow viewers to less-loaded edges without interrupting existing viewer sessions.
